display_title: 1985-01_02 — BEARING 315 Newsletter of Cascade Orienteering Club Jan/Feb 1985
body_html:
Page 1

BEARING 315
Newsletter of Cascade Orienteering Club
Jan/Feb 1985

Annual Meeting results
The slate of candidates presented in the last issue of this newsletter was elected to office without opposition at the annual meeting at 7:00 at the University branch of the Seattle Public Library on 26 November 1984. Even including votes by mail with the votes of those present resulted in less than 20 votes per candidate; a disheartening show of interest.

Our new president is Debbie Newell, vice-president is John Huth, secretary is Valerie Tinsley, and treasurer is Susan St. John. As is evident from page 2, there are still executive board member positions to be filled, so let an officer know if you are interested. Before reporting on what else happened at the meeting, I'll sketch a bit about the officers for those of you who don't know them.

Debbie has been active in local orienteering since she 1st started 5 years ago; she is one of the top cartographers around so anyone wanting to learn how to draft maps for printing may contact her. She served as vice president in 1984 and did an excellent job. With her experience and leadership, Cascade is again in good hands for the coming year.

John was mapping coordinator this past year and drafted the Lake Tradition map that was printed last summer. Even though John is one of the top course designers in the region, we can't expect him to do all the meets by himself this year so volunteer to learn course setting and develop a new skill. Designing a course and hanging the markers is a sure way to improve your orienteering. John has been active in U. S. orienteering for about 10 years starting when he was an undergraduate at SIU. In fact, he was an officer of SIUOC while there. He has been a Cascade member for 3 years now and to prove that orienteers are good athletes, he trained for and has successfully completed several tri-athlons during the past year.

Valerie has only discovered orienteering about 18 months ago but she is eager and enthusiastic as evidenced by going to the last 2 B. C. championships (and winning her class) and flying back to the North American 6-days last autumn. She also has volunteered to be on the newsletter mailing, etc. work parties.

Susan has been a member of Cascade for close to 4 years now, but her Norwegian folk-dancing activities have sometimes relegated O to 2nd place. She did quite well at our only Class A meet -- PNWOF '81, Pacific Northwest Orienteering Festival for those of you who weren't in the club then. Since we hope to receive more contributions this year, Susan should be busy with the account.
(continued on page 3)

Page 2

Officers & Board Members
The officers and executive board members of the Cascade Orienteering Club are:

PositionNamePhone

Publicity
Instruction
Promotions
Car Pools

BEARING 315 contains results, a schedule, articles of interest contributed by members or selected from other sources and lots of material written by the editor, Harvey Friedman; letters to the editor are most welcome and generally will be printed sooner or later, space permitting. Any correspondence to Cascade Orienteering Club or to this Newsletter should go to

P.O. Box 31375
Seattle, Wash. 98103

Other clubs wishing to receive BEARING 315 in exchange should send their newsletters directly to the above address.

Next Issue
All material to be published in the next issue must be received by 15 February, the earlier, the better.

Club dues
Please check the date on the mailing label to see when your membership expires. Renew promptly if you want to continue receiving BEARING 315, the club newsletter, and seeing how your name might be misspelled in the results column. An application form is enclosed somewhere in this newsletter for your convenience and also an application for the U. S. Orienteering Federation. Anyone who wants to find out about meets in other parts of the country is encouraged to join. Also to retain our charter we need a minimum of 5 USOF members in the club. Words of both thanks and welcome go to the following new or renewing club members: Boy Scout Troop 16, Troop 304, Melinda Bridgman, Robert Carlson family, DeForest Eveland, Tony Halstead, Bud Keith, Mary Ann Kerlousky, Laurie Kohli, Michael Mahoney, Gerald Nickel family, Tony Paulson, Brad Payne, Bo Powell family, Kathryn Risch, Einar Storakers.

Page 3

(continued from page 1)
To provide a service for other clubs in the area that might not have their own newsletter, it was voted to offer a new category of membership, the newsletter subscription only for $5. So, in the coming months COC members may read on a more regular basis than has occurred in the past about results of meets that they have attended in Olympia, Ellensburg, or ???.

After Mark concluded the business part of the meeting, we had a very informative and entertaining program. Surprise refreshments were provided by Anne York who brought freshly baked chocolate chip cookies (that were still warm). Dave Enger and Valerie Tinsley gave an excellent route analysis of some of their courses from the N. A. 6-day with slides of different legs of the courses so we all could see and decide for ourselves if we might have tried a different route choice. All in attendance were impressed.

Mike Schuh then presented slides from the U. S. championships in Missouri put on last November by St. Louis Orienteering Club. It looked like a lot of fun and many of us wished that we could have gotten a week-end plane flight for half of the $450 normal cut-rate fee. We couldn't even get a reduced rate for a group of 10 or more; perhaps we should try to get some airline executives interested in our tremendous sport. Anyway, 9:00 came along too soon as usual so when we had to leave the library, about a dozen of us went to get in our licks at Donna's Ice Cream place.

Scout Show '84
"I want to run the scout course", exclaimed the young cub scout as he finished the shorter Cub course. When asked why, the cub scout replied, "I've already found 4 of the markers." Another cub scout was proud to have found all the markers even though it took him 90 minutes. He probably saw a lot of other sites while making his rounds. Yet another scout finally ran the course after some arm twisting (literally) by his Jamboree Scoutmaster. Later in the day he was spotted several times at the start/finish area checking to see if anyone had bettered his time.

Boy Scout Troop 270 under the direction of Scoutmaster Fred Warden had spent the last 6 weeks preparing for their display on Orienteering at the Council's "Scout Show 1984". After deciding to offer 2 courses in addition to an information booth, the boys field-checked the area and constructed control markers using pint milk cartons and gallon milk jugs. Finally, 11 November 1984 rolled around and the scouts busied themselves putting together their display and hanging markers at Sandpoint Naval Air Station.

No records were kept on the number of courses run but it was estimated at 50+ before the rains started around 3:30 and people spent more time inside. The display of COC maps on the city map sure attracted the attention of passersby. Orienteers from COC & SOC were at hand to explain the sport to parents, scout leaders, and the older scouts who might be eligible for WIOL. Todd explained map & compass to anyone who showed an interest; he talked to so many people with so much enthusiasm that he would make a great promotions coordinator.

Page 4

As 5:00 rolled around, the scouts were anxious to see how they had scored for the display at the show. Needless to say, the ribbon and perfect score of 100% that the troop received made the dismantling of the booth much more enjoyable. The show had been a success for them and I congratulate them on a job well done. I would like to thank all the club members who showed up and helped - from COC, Harvey Friedman, Sandra Kish Miller, Robert Miller, Dave Enger, & Mike Schuh, and from SOC, Dave Enger and Bob Rein. We'll have to try it again next year.

  • John Huth

Financial Summary
Outgoing treasurer Chris Hawkins wanted the members to see the financial status of the club. Anyone who has questions should come to the 1st or 2nd meeting of 1985 when we should receive an audited financial statement.

"Classified Section"
COC IS LOOKING FOR A FEW GOOD PEOPLE
Thats right, the club is trying to move away from a "draft" to an "all volunteer" organization - so now's your chance. Some place in this issue you will find a schedule of meets planned for the upcoming year. So browse the schedule and pick out a couple of meets you would be interested in helping with. Then call me at 522-1304 after 5:30 pm to make your reservations. Don't delay; call today (or after Jan.7) so you don't miss out on this once in a lifetime opportunity. This offer is for a limited time only.

  • John Huth

SCHEDULE OF EVENTS

GENERAL INFORMATION: Unless otherwise noted, novice, intermediate and advanced courses are available at all club meets. Normal registration and start times are from 10:00 AM to NOON and entry fee is usually $1.50 for members and $2.50 for non-members. Regional and National (Class 'B' and Class 'A') events may have a higher entry fee depending upon sanctions and awards given to winners in various categories. Instruction for beginners is always available at all events. A limited number of compasses are available on a rental basis at registration.

\ 2nd course tried*

The snowing and possible icing made many leery about trying to drive to the meet. Those who were there had fun running thru the swirling snow; there were tracks in the fields but not in the forest, Pies were enjoyed at Debbie & Arnold's as usual. -HF

Pot-O'-luck
The next Cascade O.C. potluck will be at a location to be announced at the 22 Jan club meeting. Anyone who has not yet hosted a potluck and who wants to should contact Harvey. As usually occurs at these potlucks, the food should again be excellent and you have the opportunity to socialize and talk about something other than orienteering ( or you can talk about orienteering ).

Cascade Chatter
2 Cascade members, Jan Vrana and Jessica Hirschfelder, completed the recent Seattle marathon the weekend after Thanksgiving. Though both were happy to finish, neither was satisfied with the time. The cold and wind were the problems everyone at that race....Stein Sandven, Cascade member living in Bergen, Norway (actually he is a IK Gular member who joined Cascade for 9 months when he was here 3 years ago) spent a week in Seattle 14-21 Dec after attending scientific meetings in S. F. and San Diego. Though we had no meets scheduled then, he was able to see or speak with several of his old friends. He plans on attending both the JK and the Scottish 6-day so anyone who goes to those meets can renew old friendship or if you had never met him, you'll know who that guy in the Cascade O. C. t-shirt is....Mark Donovan of 3ROC of Dublin, Ireland who was here for the summer of 1983 and helped with field-checking Lake Tradition is now studying for a Ph. D. in Molecular Biology at
(continued on page 10)

Page 10

Wesleyan University, Conn. His address is 3 Brainerd Ave., Middletown, Conn 06457....We finally have an address for Ingemar Jansson -- c/o Haverstad, Somveien 78, N-0493 Oslo 4, Norway....We have an application for the Belgian 3-day, 25-27 May. anyone interested, contact Harvey....The Danish 3-day will be 5-7 July; we don't have apllications but anyone interested should ask Harvey how to get one....Several of us will be going to the Swedish 5-day, 22-26 July and to the Scottish 6-day, 4-10 August. Contact Harvey. Closer to home, the Canadian O' champs will be 27 July- 4 August in Alberta; this will be a lot of fun and anyone not going to Europe but who wants an excellent quality meet and a chance to meet internationals should plan on attending....Even closer to home, the Nisqually Orienteers will hold a Class 'A' meet in late August or early September of 1985. We should try to have 100 or more Cascade members attend because it's sure to be a fine meet....Closer on the calendar, but slightly farther away, the Ellensburg Orienteering Club will hold their 3rd annual Kittitas Valley O' Festival at Cle Elum Ridge. This will be a Class 'B' meet. Be there...Anyone interested in orienteering in Oregon can write to Oregon Orienteering Services, 4349 SW Garden Home Rd., Portland OR 97219 Meets are scheduled for 12 Jan at Oak Creek, 2 Feb at Berry Creek, and 3 Mar at Chip Ross Park but we have no phone number for them.... Club members who were on the mailing list as of last October should have recived a sample copy of Orienteering North America, a new magazine published on the East Coast. Anyone who wants more O' news than you find here or in OUSA ( the journal of the U. S. O. federation) should consider subscribing. I have.

Apologia
In the Sep/Oct issue of BEARING 315, an error was made in the results for the state meet. Francis Smith was incorrectly reported as having taken 4 hours to complete the white course. We seem to have lost his actual time, but in 4 hours he comoleted the white course, went back out and picked up the control markers, drove home, and cleaned up and showered. His enthusiastic help was most welcome at the meet and we are sorry for the misinformation.

Tentative schedule for 1985
In addition to the meets listed in the regular schedule of this issue, we are considering: Sat 6 Apr Volunteer Park Bunnie-O; Sat 27 Apr Lake Tradition; Sun 19 May Foster Is./U. W. Canoe-o/Bike-O; Sat 1 June Hamlin Park; Sat 22 Jun Lincoln Park relay; Fri 5 July Woodland Park 6:00 pm picnic-score; Sat 20 Jul Edmonds S.W. Co. Park; Sun 11 Aug Ballard bike-O; ?? Aug Crystal springs Class A or B; Sat/Sun 14/15 Sep Mt. Tumac mountain O; Sat 5 Oct Cle Elum; Sat 26 Oct Sand Pt. 5:00 pm ghost-o; Sat 16 Nov Crystal Springs ski-O; Sat 7 Dec ??? WIOL meet; Sat 28 Dec St. Edwards 6th annual holiday bog slog;

BEARING 315 will continue to publish upcoming events in each issue with specifics regarding dates, times, places, and info phone number. I hope to have several other meets added through the year depending on enthusiasm of club members. Conversely, if interest lags and help is not available for the scheduled meets, some may be CANCELED. Hope to see you in the woods, just don't follow me.

  • John Huth

Page 11

The French 5-days: a personal perspective
by TERI CHERESKIN (reprinted from the Fall Oregon O newsletter)

More than 3000 competitors from 24 countries attended this year's French 5-days held in the forest of Aquitaine near Bordeaux. All in all, 7 different maps were produced for the event since 2 training days preceded the 5 competition days (4-8 July).

The enormous forest of Aquitaine is almost entirely man-made, created to stabilize the sand dunes. A mere 150 years ago the countryside was seascape; now there is forest bordered on the west by a 270 km stretch of sandy beach and large dunes. Two types of dunes (white, than gray) protect the forest from the sea, and they are separated by "lettes" or lowlands covered with low vegetation: briar, thorns, bracken, and small pines. Here the running was difficult and relief intricate. The forest proper was beautiful, open pine...very fast and runnable. Numerous firebreaks squared off the terrain, making good catching features but not ideal routes since the loose sand made for tiring running. The other open areas (deforested/reforested regions) were also difficult to run through due to loose sand and undergrowth.

1st starts were at 7am; last starts were at noon. A late start was a real penalty since it got very hot (in the 90's). 3 finish funnels were used to handle all the people. Day One was the World Cup competition day. Finish photos of all the competitors were taken and a film was shot. The finishes for the 1st 2 days were on the beach and involved running 80 m or so through loose sand over large dunes. It was a bizarre sight to see people in brightly colored orienteering kit running with map and compass onto a French beach where most people were wearing almost nothing at all. It wasn't long, though, before the finishers joined them. The finish on Day Three was also close to the ocean while on Day Four we finished on a large lake. The finish for Day Five was at the town center of Maubisson, a holiday resort which had graciously hosted us throughout the event. By Day Five the locals had become used to orienteers thronging their streets.

It was a truly decadent experience to combine morning after morning of orienteering with afternoons of body-surfing, windsurfing, swimming, drinking, eating, and merrymaking. The organizers of the French 5-days are to be commended for hosting such a well-run and enjoyable meet.

Newsletter Subscription for $5
A subscription to BEARING 315 ( but without the other benefits of membership) is now available for $5 per year. This is a great opportunity to give an inexpensive gift to your friends and/or relatives whom you might want to interest in trying the sport of orienteering. Though we would prefer not to lose any regular members, for those of you who attend 2 meets or less per year, we realize that some folks might want to save their money by subscribing. That is OK. But, we must now be more intolerant of late renewals. If you don't renew, by the expiration date, on the label (or before the next issue is in the mail), you won't get the next issue. YOU HAVE BEEN WARNED.

Page 12

block_index.text: BEARING 315 Newsletter of Cascade Orienteering Club Jan/Feb 1985

Annual Meeting results The slate of candidates presented in the last issue of this newsletter was elected to office without opposition at the annual meeting at 7:00 at the University branch of the Seattle Public Library on 26 November 1984. Even including votes by mail with the votes of those present resulted in less than 20 votes per candidate; a disheartening show of interest.

Our new president is Debbie Newell, vice-president is John Huth, secretary is Valerie Tinsley, and treasurer is Susan St. John. As is evident from page 2, there are still executive board member positions to be filled, so let an officer know if you are interested. Before reporting on what else happened at the meeting, I'll sketch a bit about the officers for those of you who don't know them.

Debbie has been active in local orienteering since she 1st started 5 years ago; she is one of the top cartographers around so anyone wanting to learn how to draft maps for printing may contact her. She served as vice president in 1984 and did an excellent job. With her experience and leadership, Cascade is again in good hands for the coming year.

John was mapping coordinator this past year and drafted the Lake Tradition map that was printed last summer. Even though John is one of the top course designers in the region, we can't expect him to do all the meets by himself this year so volunteer to learn course setting and develop a new skill. Designing a course and hanging the markers is a sure way to improve your orienteering. John has been active in U. S. orienteering for about 10 years starting when he was an undergraduate at SIU. In fact, he was an officer of SIUOC while there. He has been a Cascade member for 3 years now and to prove that orienteers are good athletes, he trained for and has successfully completed several tri-athlons during the past year.

Valerie has only discovered orienteering about 18 months ago but she is eager and enthusiastic as evidenced by going to the last 2 B. C. championships (and winning her class) and flying back to the North American 6-days last autumn. She also has volunteered to be on the newsletter mailing, etc. work parties.

Susan has been a member of Cascade for close to 4 years now, but her Norwegian folk-dancing activities have sometimes relegated O to 2nd place. She did quite well at our only Class A meet -- PNWOF '81, Pacific Northwest Orienteering Festival for those of you who weren't in the club then. Since we hope to receive more contributions this year, Susan should be busy with the account. (continued on page 3)

Club dues Please check the date on the mailing label to see when your membership expires. Renew promptly if you want to continue receiving BEARING 315, the club newsletter, and seeing how your name might be misspelled in the results column. An application form is enclosed somewhere in this newsletter for your convenience and also an application for the U. S. Orienteering Federation. Anyone who wants to find out about meets in other parts of the country is encouraged to join. Also to retain our charter we need a minimum of 5 USOF members in the club. Words of both thanks and welcome go to the following new or renewing club members: Boy Scout Troop 16, Troop 304, Melinda Bridgman, Robert Carlson family, DeForest Eveland, Tony Halstead, Bud Keith, Mary Ann Kerlousky, Laurie Kohli, Michael Mahoney, Gerald Nickel family, Tony Paulson, Brad Payne, Bo Powell family, Kathryn Risch, Einar Storakers.

(continued from page 1) To provide a service for other clubs in the area that might not have their own newsletter, it was voted to offer a new category of membership, the newsletter subscription only for $5. So, in the coming months COC members may read on a more regular basis than has occurred in the past about results of meets that they have attended in Olympia, Ellensburg, or ???.

After Mark concluded the business part of the meeting, we had a very informative and entertaining program. Surprise refreshments were provided by Anne York who brought freshly baked chocolate chip cookies (that were still warm). Dave Enger and Valerie Tinsley gave an excellent route analysis of some of their courses from the N. A. 6-day with slides of different legs of the courses so we all could see and decide for ourselves if we might have tried a different route choice. All in attendance were impressed.

Mike Schuh then presented slides from the U. S. championships in Missouri put on last November by St. Louis Orienteering Club. It looked like a lot of fun and many of us wished that we could have gotten a week-end plane flight for half of the $450 normal cut-rate fee. We couldn't even get a reduced rate for a group of 10 or more; perhaps we should try to get some airline executives interested in our tremendous sport. Anyway, 9:00 came along too soon as usual so when we had to leave the library, about a dozen of us went to get in our licks at Donna's Ice Cream place.

Scout Show '84 "I want to run the scout course", exclaimed the young cub scout as he finished the shorter Cub course. When asked why, the cub scout replied, "I've already found 4 of the markers." Another cub scout was proud to have found all the markers even though it took him 90 minutes. He probably saw a lot of other sites while making his rounds. Yet another scout finally ran the course after some arm twisting (literally) by his Jamboree Scoutmaster. Later in the day he was spotted several times at the start/finish area checking to see if anyone had bettered his time.

Boy Scout Troop 270 under the direction of Scoutmaster Fred Warden had spent the last 6 weeks preparing for their display on Orienteering at the Council's "Scout Show 1984". After deciding to offer 2 courses in addition to an information booth, the boys field-checked the area and constructed control markers using pint milk cartons and gallon milk jugs. Finally, 11 November 1984 rolled around and the scouts busied themselves putting together their display and hanging markers at Sandpoint Naval Air Station.

No records were kept on the number of courses run but it was estimated at 50+ before the rains started around 3:30 and people spent more time inside. The display of COC maps on the city map sure attracted the attention of passersby. Orienteers from COC & SOC were at hand to explain the sport to parents, scout leaders, and the older scouts who might be eligible for WIOL. Todd explained map & compass to anyone who showed an interest; he talked to so many people with so much enthusiasm that he would make a great promotions coordinator.

As 5:00 rolled around, the scouts were anxious to see how they had scored for the display at the show. Needless to say, the ribbon and perfect score of 100% that the troop received made the dismantling of the booth much more enjoyable. The show had been a success for them and I congratulate them on a job well done. I would like to thank all the club members who showed up and helped - from COC, Harvey Friedman, Sandra Kish Miller, Robert Miller, Dave Enger, & Mike Schuh, and from SOC, Dave Enger and Bob Rein. We'll have to try it again next year.

Financial Summary Outgoing treasurer Chris Hawkins wanted the members to see the financial status of the club. Anyone who has questions should come to the 1st or 2nd meeting of 1985 when we should receive an audited financial statement.

"Classified Section" COC IS LOOKING FOR A FEW GOOD PEOPLE Thats right, the club is trying to move away from a "draft" to an "all volunteer" organization - so now's your chance. Then call me at 522-1304 after 5:30 pm to make your reservations. Don't delay; call today (or after Jan.7) so you don't miss out on this once in a lifetime opportunity. This offer is for a limited time only.

GENERAL INFORMATION: Unless otherwise noted, novice, intermediate and advanced courses are available at all club meets. Normal registration and start times are from 10:00 AM to NOON and entry fee is usually $1.50 for members and $2.50 for non-members. Regional and National (Class 'B' and Class 'A') events may have a higher entry fee depending upon sanctions and awards given to winners in various categories. Instruction for beginners is always available at all events. A limited number of compasses are available on a rental basis at registration.

Pot-O'-luck The next Cascade O.C. potluck will be at a location to be announced at the 22 Jan club meeting. Anyone who has not yet hosted a potluck and who wants to should contact Harvey. As usually occurs at these potlucks, the food should again be excellent and you have the opportunity to socialize and talk about something other than orienteering ( or you can talk about orienteering ).

Cascade Chatter 2 Cascade members, Jan Vrana and Jessica Hirschfelder, completed the recent Seattle marathon the weekend after Thanksgiving. Though both were happy to finish, neither was satisfied with the time. The cold and wind were the problems everyone at that race....Stein Sandven, Cascade member living in Bergen, Norway (actually he is a IK Gular member who joined Cascade for 9 months when he was here 3 years ago) spent a week in Seattle 14-21 Dec after attending scientific meetings in S. F. and San Diego. Though we had no meets scheduled then, he was able to see or speak with several of his old friends. He plans on attending both the JK and the Scottish 6-day so anyone who goes to those meets can renew old friendship or if you had never met him, you'll know who that guy in the Cascade O. C. t-shirt is....Mark Donovan of 3ROC of Dublin, Ireland who was here for the summer of 1983 and helped with field-checking Lake Tradition is now studying for a Ph. D. in Molecular Biology at (continued on page 10)

Wesleyan University, Conn. His address is 3 Brainerd Ave., Middletown, Conn 06457....We finally have an address for Ingemar Jansson -- c/o Haverstad, Somveien 78, N-0493 Oslo 4, Norway....We have an application for the Belgian 3-day, 25-27 May. anyone interested, contact Harvey....The Danish 3-day will be 5-7 July; we don't have apllications but anyone interested should ask Harvey how to get one....Several of us will be going to the Swedish 5-day, 22-26 July and to the Scottish 6-day, 4-10 August. Contact Harvey. Closer to home, the Canadian O' champs will be 27 July- 4 August in Alberta; this will be a lot of fun and anyone not going to Europe but who wants an excellent quality meet and a chance to meet internationals should plan on attending....Even closer to home, the Nisqually Orienteers will hold a Class 'A' meet in late August or early September of 1985. We should try to have 100 or more Cascade members attend because it's sure to be a fine meet....Closer on the calendar, but slightly farther away, the Ellensburg Orienteering Club will hold their 3rd annual Kittitas Valley O' Festival at Cle Elum Ridge. This will be a Class 'B' meet. Be there...Anyone interested in orienteering in Oregon can write to Oregon Orienteering Services, 4349 SW Garden Home Rd., Portland OR 97219 Meets are scheduled for 12 Jan at Oak Creek, 2 Feb at Berry Creek, and 3 Mar at Chip Ross Park but we have no phone number for them.... Club members who were on the mailing list as of last October should have recived a sample copy of Orienteering North America, a new magazine published on the East Coast. Anyone who wants more O' news than you find here or in OUSA ( the journal of the U. S. O. federation) should consider subscribing. I have.

Apologia In the Sep/Oct issue of BEARING 315, an error was made in the results for the state meet. Francis Smith was incorrectly reported as having taken 4 hours to complete the white course. We seem to have lost his actual time, but in 4 hours he comoleted the white course, went back out and picked up the control markers, drove home, and cleaned up and showered. His enthusiastic help was most welcome at the meet and we are sorry for the misinformation.

The French 5-days: a personal perspective by TERI CHERESKIN (reprinted from the Fall Oregon O newsletter)

More than 3000 competitors from 24 countries attended this year's French 5-days held in the forest of Aquitaine near Bordeaux. All in all, 7 different maps were produced for the event since 2 training days preceded the 5 competition days (4-8 July).

The enormous forest of Aquitaine is almost entirely man-made, created to stabilize the sand dunes. A mere 150 years ago the countryside was seascape; now there is forest bordered on the west by a 270 km stretch of sandy beach and large dunes. Two types of dunes (white, than gray) protect the forest from the sea, and they are separated by "lettes" or lowlands covered with low vegetation: briar, thorns, bracken, and small pines. Here the running was difficult and relief intricate. The forest proper was beautiful, open pine...very fast and runnable. Numerous firebreaks squared off the terrain, making good catching features but not ideal routes since the loose sand made for tiring running. The other open areas (deforested/reforested regions) were also difficult to run through due to loose sand and undergrowth.

1st starts were at 7am; last starts were at noon. A late start was a real penalty since it got very hot (in the 90's). 3 finish funnels were used to handle all the people. Day One was the World Cup competition day. Finish photos of all the competitors were taken and a film was shot. The finishes for the 1st 2 days were on the beach and involved running 80 m or so through loose sand over large dunes. It was a bizarre sight to see people in brightly colored orienteering kit running with map and compass onto a French beach where most people were wearing almost nothing at all. It wasn't long, though, before the finishers joined them. The finish on Day Three was also close to the ocean while on Day Four we finished on a large lake. The finish for Day Five was at the town center of Maubisson, a holiday resort which had graciously hosted us throughout the event. By Day Five the locals had become used to orienteers thronging their streets.

It was a truly decadent experience to combine morning after morning of orienteering with afternoons of body-surfing, windsurfing, swimming, drinking, eating, and merrymaking. The organizers of the French 5-days are to be commended for hosting such a well-run and enjoyable meet.

Newsletter Subscription for $5 A subscription to BEARING 315 ( but without the other benefits of membership) is now available for $5 per year. This is a great opportunity to give an inexpensive gift to your friends and/or relatives whom you might want to interest in trying the sport of orienteering. Though we would prefer not to lose any regular members, for those of you who attend 2 meets or less per year, we realize that some folks might want to save their money by subscribing. That is OK. But, we must now be more intolerant of late renewals. If you don't renew, by the expiration date, on the label (or before the next issue is in the mail), you won't get the next issue. YOU HAVE BEEN WARNED.
